hook: A 1653 Flemish stock exchange of 24 houses round a courtyard, now a bouquinistes' den.
facts: Built 1652-1653 by master mason Julien Destree
Twenty-four identical houses enclose one courtyard
Authorised 1651 by Philip IV of Spain
Now hosts secondhand book stalls, flower sellers and chess players
w: A box-maker by trade built this in 1653, and it shows: twenty-four merchant houses lacquered with caryatids and cartouches, packed tight as a jewel case round one courtyard. The Spanish wanted a bourse; Lille got a Flemish reliquary. Now bouquinistes and chess sharks haggle where coin once changed hands.

hook: A 1237 countess's hospital, its sick ward now hung with Flemish art and Delft tiles.
facts: Founded 1237 by Jeanne, Countess of Flanders
Functioned as a hospital and hospice until 1939
Great sick ward dates from the 15th century
Now a museum of Lille's art and history with Flemish paintings and faience
legend: Jeanne is said to have founded it in penance, having let her own husband languish in captivity
w: Countess Jeanne raised this hospital in 1237, legend says in penance for leaving her husband to rot in a French jail. The sick lay under a vaulted timber ceiling like an upturned hull; nuns tended them until 1939. Now Delft tiles and Flemish canvases line the ward. Charity, beautifully preserved.

hook: Guimard's only northern building: an Art Nouveau facade in green enamelled lava, wedged between neighbours.
facts: Designed by Hector Guimard, completed around 1900
Built for ceramics dealer Louis Coilliot as showroom and home
Facade clad in green enamelled lava stone from Volvic
Stands at 14 rue de Fleurus; a private residence, not open inside
w: Guimard, the man who gave Paris its Metro signs, came north just once, building a showroom-home for a ceramics dealer around 1900. The facade writhes in wrought iron and green enamelled lava, set at a sly angle to its prim brick neighbours. Easy to miss on rue de Fleurus. Then impossible to unsee.

hook: A lone 1402 Burgundian rampart tower, now a memorial holding ashes from the camps.
facts: Built 1402 under Philippe le Hardi, Duke of Burgundy
Once the tallest of the city wall's 65 towers
Walls three metres thick, banked with earth against fire
Made a Resistance and Deportation memorial, inaugurated by de Gaulle in 1959
w: All that survives of Lille's 65 medieval towers, this squat Burgundian stump from 1402 once stood tallest, walls three metres thick and packed with earth against fire. Vauban kept it; gunpowder filled it. Now an urn inside holds ashes from the death camps, and de Gaulle came to bless the silence in 1959.

hook: An 1865 goods station, abandoned, now a free arts venue with bistro and rooftop farm.
facts: Opened 1865 as a freight-only station
Closed and left abandoned in 2003
Reopened 2009 for Europe XXL as a cultural venue
Houses exhibitions, a cinema, the Bistrot de St-So and a seasonal urban farm
w: Freight only, never passengers: from 1865 this shed handled coal and crates, then sat dead from 2003. The city handed the brick hangars to artists in 2009. Now exhibitions hang where wagons rolled, a cinema flickers, and a seasonal farm sprouts on the roof. The Bistrot de St-So pours till late.

hook: A neo-Gothic cathedral begun 1854, finally capped in 1999 by a glowing veil of thin marble.
facts: Construction began 1854, the west front completed only in 1999
West facade designed by Pierre-Louis Carlier with engineer Peter Rice
Central oculus made of translucent Portuguese marble, 15 tonnes at 3 cm thick
Inside, the marble backlights a fiery orange glow
w: They started building in 1854 and ran out of nerve, leaving a brick stump for over a century. The fix, finished 1999, defies its Gothic shell: 110 sheets of marble shaved to three centimetres, blank grey from the street. Step inside at the right hour and the whole west wall ignites orange. A burning bush in stone.

hook: A 1620 Spanish-era gate guarding rue de Gand, Lille's row of brick-and-beer estaminets.
facts: Built 1620 by Pierre Raoul during the Spanish period
Also known as the Porte de la Madeleine
Vauban added outer hornwork, traces still in the gardens
Inner facade in polychrome herringbone brick
w: Spanish Lille threw up this gate in 1620, all stern grey stone meant to cow attackers; turn to its inner face and the brick breaks into cheerful herringbone. Vauban later bolted on outer earthworks. Behind it, rue de Gand runs as a tunnel of estaminets, the carbonnade thick and the beer northern.

hook: A brick-walled Flemish tavern named for Lille's own Dutch name, all stew and local beer.
facts: Named 'T Rijsel, the Flemish name for Lille
Sits at 25 rue de Gand in Vieux-Lille
Serves carbonnade flamande and Flemish pot-au-feu of four meats
Pours regional brews including Ch'ti
w: The name says everything: 'T Rijsel, what the Flemish called Lille before France took it. Inside, brick walls, board games, and carbonnade that surrenders at the touch of a fork, beef simmered in dark beer till it forgets it was ever tough. A four-meat pot-au-feu for the brave. Order the Ch'ti and stay.

hook: A dead textile mill reborn in 2004 as a scrappy arts house with a metal-skinned hall.
facts: A former textile mill at 70 rue des Sarrazins
Reopened 2004 for Lille European Capital of Culture
Reworked by Dutch architect Lars Spuybroek of agency Nox
Old brick kept beside a new metal-clad multi-purpose hall
w: Wazemmes ran on textiles until the looms went quiet, leaving brick sheds to rot. For Lille's culture-capital year a Dutch architect kept the old walls and slid a shimmering metal hall between them. Now dance, cabaret and workshops fill a working-class quarter's old mill. Industry, handed to the artists.

hook: Lille's loudest, most polyglot market, its Sunday sprawl flooding the streets around the hall.
facts: Centred on the covered hall near place de la Nouvelle Aventure
Open several mornings weekly, peaking Sunday
Outdoor stalls spill far beyond the hall on market days
Known for North African, Flemish and produce stalls side by side
w: On Sundays the covered hall can't hold it: stalls burst into the surrounding streets, the racket of Arabic, Flemish and ch'ti haggling rising over mint, merguez and mussels. This was always the poor immigrant quarter, and the market still tastes of every wave that landed. Come hungry, leave shoved.

hook: Vauban's five-pointed star fort, the 'Queen of Citadels', still army-held and ringed by woods.
facts: Built 1667-1670 by Vauban after Louis XIV took Lille
A perfect pentagon star fort, called the Queen of Citadels
Some 60 million bricks went into its construction
Still a working military base; surrounded by the Bois de Boulogne park
w: When Louis XIV seized Lille in 1667, Vauban answered with his masterpiece: a five-pointed brick star, sixty million bricks, that he himself called the Queen of Citadels. Three centuries on it's still an army base, off-limits at its heart. Lillois jog the ramparts and lose themselves in the encircling woods.

hook: A triumphal arch grafted onto a city gate, flattering Louis XIV for taking the town in 1667.
facts: Built 1685-1692 by Simon Vollant, a collaborator of Vauban
Marks the 1667 conquest of Lille by Louis XIV
Combines a defensive gate with a Roman-style triumphal arch
Topped by figures of Victory crowning the king
w: Most cities raise a triumphal arch to their own glory. Lille's, built by Vauban's man Vollant in the 1680s, celebrates the king who conquered it, Louis XIV, all Victories and trumpets carved to flatter the new master. A gate and a boast in one. The losers paid for the winner's monument.

hook: One of France's biggest art museums, with Goya witches and 3D relief maps of fortified towns.
facts: Housed in a grand Belle Epoque palace opened 1892
Among the largest fine-arts museums in France
Holds Goya's pair of allegories of Youth and Old Age
Basement displays 18th-century scale relief maps of northern fortress towns
w: Skip the grand staircase and head down: in the basement crouch the plans-reliefs, tabletop models of fortified towns built so kings could plot sieges without leaving Versailles. Upstairs, Goya's witches leer beside Flemish masters. One of France's deepest collections, and barely a tour bus in sight.

hook: The old mint street, a curve of 17th-18th century brick gables in the heart of Vieux-Lille.
facts: Named for the royal mint that operated here
Lined with restored brick and stone Flemish facades
Runs through the medieval core toward the Hospice Comtesse
Among the oldest surviving streets in Lille
w: The street where Lille once struck its coin, the mint long gone but the name kept. Its curve of stepped brick gables, restored after decades as a slum, runs past the Hospice Comtesse into the oldest tangle of the city. Look up: every facade carries a different date, a different merchant's pride.

n: Maison Natale de Charles de Gaulle
hook: The bourgeois townhouse where de Gaulle was born in 1890, kept as his family knew it.
facts: Birthplace of Charles de Gaulle, born 22 November 1890
His grandmother's house at 9 rue Princesse
Family returned here through his childhood
Now a museum with period rooms and a modern visitor centre
w: Behind a sober brick front on rue Princesse, the general drew his first breath in 1890, in his grandmother's house above the family lace business. The rooms stay as they were: piano, prie-dieu, the baptismal robe. France's most towering figure of the century began as a Lille child in the parlour. Modest beginnings.

hook: A rare Flemish hall-church, five aisles of equal height built on marshy ground over 500 years.
facts: A hallekerque or hall-church, all five naves the same height
Built and extended from the 14th to the 19th century
Stands on former marshland, set on wooden piles
Slender pillars create an unusually open, bright interior
w: A Flemish hallekerque, five naves of equal height so the inside feels like a stone forest rather than a tunnel. Begun in the 14th century, finished only in the 19th, it stands on wooden piles driven into old marsh. Slender pillars, flooding light. Near the station, yet most hurry past the door.
